sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=31, col=43).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 18, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking green+c at right frame swapped colors (left now c, right now W) but bar stayed at 10. Previously clicking green+W at left frame extended bar but didn't swap. Hypothesis: clicking a green square with WHITE center at a frame extends the progress bar, while clicking non-white swaps colors. Let me test by clicking the right green (now W center) at the right frame.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 17 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The bar extended from 10 to 11 pixels! And the centers stayed the same (left c, right W). So my hypothesis is confirmed:
- Clicking a green square with WHITE center at a frame position extends the bar by 1
- Clicking a green square with non-white center at a frame position swaps the colors
Now I need to figure out the goal. The bar is at 11 pixels. I need to keep clicking green squares with W centers at frame positions to extend the bar further. The question is: how long does the bar need to be to complete the level?
Current state:
- Left green: center c (at left frame)
- Right green: center W (at right frame)
- Bar: 11 pixels
I can click the right green (W center) at the right frame again to extend the bar. But I need to know how many times I need to do this.
Wait, but I already clicked the right green (W center) at the right frame and the bar extended. The centers stayed the same. So I can keep clicking the same green square at the same frame to keep extending the bar.
Let me try clicking the right green (W center) at the right frame multiple times to see how fast the bar fills up and when the level completes.
Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The bar is at row 63, extending from col 53 to col 63 (11 pixels). The grid is 64 columns wide. If the bar needs to fill the entire width, it would need to extend from col 0 to col 63, which is 64 pixels. That would take 64 - 11 = 53 more clicks. That seems like a lot.
But wait, maybe the bar doesn't need to fill the entire width. Maybe it needs to reach a certain length. Let me look at the initial bar length.
From the first action result, the bar was at [[63, 55], [63, 63]], which is 9 pixels. After my first frame click, it became 10 pixels. After the second, 11 pixels. So each frame click with W center adds 1 pixel.
Actually, I should check the very initial state (before any actions) to see the starting bar length. Let me check history[0].
Actually, I don't need to be that precise. Let me just keep clicking the right green (W center) at the right frame to extend the bar and see when the level completes. I can batch multiple clicks.
But wait, there's a more efficient approach. What if I need to click BOTH frames? Maybe clicking the left frame with a W center extends the bar by a different amount, or maybe I need to click each frame a certain number of times.
Let me try a different strategy: click the right green (W) at the right frame several times in a batch to see how the bar progresses.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Confirmed! Clicking green+W at frame extends bar by 1, clicking green+c at frame swaps colors. Bar is now 11 pixels. I can keep clicking the right green (W center) at the right frame to extend the bar. Let me batch several clicks to fill the bar faster.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me batch click the right green (W center) at the right frame multiple times
# to extend the bar and see when the level completes
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 43}] * 10)
print("Result:", result)

# Check bar length
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and 8 <= n['pixels'] <= 20:
        print(f"Bar: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['pixels'] == 1:
        print(f"Center: color={n['color']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
